walkthrough.title: Choose Your Mages
walkthrough.text: Let's pick our mages. Each player selects one mage character card. Every mage has a unique ability, a special charge power, and a different starting hand and breach configuration. For our first game, let's go with two players. Grab two mage mats and place them in front of you. These mats are your command center for the whole game.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Mage Starting Decks
walkthrough.text: Each mage starts with a small personal deck. Check your mage card for the exact starting cards. Most mages begin with a mix of basic gems and basic spells. Shuffle your starting deck and place it face down to the left of your mage mat. This is your personal draw pile.

walkthrough.title: Draw Your Opening Hand
walkthrough.text: Each player draws five cards from their personal deck. This is your opening hand. Take a look at what you have. You will probably see a few Crystal gems and maybe a Spark spell. These are your humble beginnings. By the end of the game, your deck will be loaded with powerful spells and upgraded gems.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Your Breaches
walkthrough.text: Each mage mat shows four breaches numbered one through four. Breach one starts open. Breaches two, three, and four start closed for most mages. Some mages have different breach configurations, so check your mage card. Closed breaches are positioned face down. Open breaches are face up. You can only prep spells on open breaches.

walkthrough.title: Choose the Nemesis
walkthrough.text: Now let's pick our enemy. Choose one nemesis from the box. For a first game, Rageborne is a great starter boss. Take the nemesis mat and place it in the center of the table. Set the nemesis hit point dial to the value shown on the nemesis card. Rageborne starts at seventy hit points.

walkthrough.title: Build the Nemesis Deck
walkthrough.text: Each nemesis has its own set of cards. Separate the nemesis cards into three tiers: tier one, tier two, and tier three. Shuffle each tier separately. Then stack them with tier one on top, tier two in the middle, and tier three on the bottom. This ensures the nemesis starts manageable and escalates as the game goes on.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Gravehold
walkthrough.text: Gravehold is the city we are all defending. Place the Gravehold life dial where everyone can see it and set it to thirty. If Gravehold reaches zero, we all lose. Protecting Gravehold is just as important as damaging the nemesis. Every point of city health matters.

walkthrough.title: Create the Supply — Gems
walkthrough.text: Let's build the market. Choose three gem piles for the supply. For a first game, a good mix is one cheap gem, one mid-cost gem, and one expensive gem. Place them in a row where everyone can reach them. Gems generate Aether, which is the currency you spend to buy new cards and open breaches.

walkthrough.title: Create the Supply — Relics
walkthrough.text: Choose three relic piles for the supply. Relics are one-time-use cards with powerful effects like healing, extra draws, or bonus damage. Place them next to the gem piles. Together with the gems, these form the first six of your nine supply piles.

walkthrough.title: Create the Supply — Spells
walkthrough.text: Choose three spell piles to complete the supply. Spells are your primary weapons against the nemesis. They must be prepped on open breaches and then cast on your next turn. Pick a variety of damage levels. Place them beside the relics. That completes all nine supply piles.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Turn Order Deck
walkthrough.text: Find the turn order cards. These determine who goes next each round, and that includes the nemesis. The turn order deck contains two player turn cards and two nemesis turn cards for a two-player game. Shuffle them face down. This deck is what makes every round unpredictable. You never know if the nemesis will strike once or twice before you get to act.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Tokens and Begin
walkthrough.text: Set out any charge tokens and damage tokens near the play area. Each mage has a charge track on their mat. Place the charge marker at zero. Make sure everyone can see the nemesis hit points and Gravehold hit points. Flip the first turn order card and let's begin. We are the last line of defense for Gravehold.

summary.title: Create the Supply — Gems

Healing, extra draws, bonus damage, etc.

summary.title: Shuffle the Turn Order Deck
bullets: 2-player: 2 player cards + 2 nemesis cards

summary.title: Prepare Tokens and Begin
bullets: Set out charge tokens and damage tokens
Charge markers start at 0 on each mage mat
Flip first turn order card → begin

walkthrough.title: The Turn Order Deck
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about what makes this game so tense. At the start of each round, we flip one turn order card at a time. That card tells us whether a player or the nemesis acts next. In a two-player game, the deck has four cards: two player turns and two nemesis turns. We never know the order until we flip. The nemesis could go twice in a row before we act.

walkthrough.title: Player Turn Overview
walkthrough.text: When a player turn card is flipped, one of us takes a full turn. A player turn has three phases in order: the Casting Phase, the Main Phase, and the Draw Phase. Let's walk through each one. We will always follow this order, and we cannot skip back to an earlier phase once we move on.

walkthrough.title: Casting Phase
walkthrough.text: The Casting Phase comes first. Any spells you prepped on your breaches last turn now fire. Resolve each prepped spell, dealing its damage to the nemesis or its minions. After a spell is cast, it goes to your discard pile. If you have no prepped spells, just skip this phase. Casting is automatic. You must cast all prepped spells.

walkthrough.title: Main Phase — Playing Gems and Relics
walkthrough.text: Now we enter the Main Phase. This is where most of your decisions happen. You can play gem cards from your hand to generate Aether. Aether is the currency for everything: buying new cards from the supply, focusing breaches, opening breaches, and more. You can also play relic cards for their special effects. Play as many gems and relics as you want in any order.

walkthrough.title: Main Phase — Prepping Spells
walkthrough.text: During your Main Phase, you can prep spells from your hand onto your open breaches. Each open breach holds one spell at a time. Place the spell card on the breach. It will fire automatically at the start of your next turn during your Casting Phase. You cannot prep spells on closed breaches. Planning which spells to prep and where is a huge part of the strategy.

walkthrough.title: Main Phase — Focusing and Opening Breaches
walkthrough.text: Closed breaches must be focused before they can be opened. Focusing a breach means paying Aether to rotate it one step closer to open. Each breach shows its focus cost on the closed side. Once a breach has been fully focused, you can pay its open cost to flip it face up permanently. Opening a breach is a big investment, but it gives you another slot to prep spells for the rest of the game.

walkthrough.title: Main Phase — Buying Cards
walkthrough.text: Let's spend that Aether. You can buy cards from any of the nine supply piles by paying their Aether cost. Purchased cards go directly to your discard pile, not your hand. You will draw them in future turns. Buying better gems improves your economy. Buying powerful spells increases your damage output. Buying relics gives you flexibility. Every purchase shapes your deck for the rest of the game.

walkthrough.title: Main Phase — Charge Abilities
walkthrough.text: Each mage has a unique charge ability shown on their mage mat. During your Main Phase, you can spend Aether to gain one charge. When your charge track is full, you can activate your charge ability. These abilities are extremely powerful. Some deal massive damage, some heal Gravehold, some let you draw extra cards. Building charges over several turns and then unleashing your ability at the right moment can turn the game around.

walkthrough.title: Draw Phase — The Discard Order Trick
walkthrough.text: Here is what makes this game truly unique. After your Main Phase, you discard all remaining cards from your hand and then draw five new cards. But here is the critical part: you choose the order you discard. Why does this matter? Because in this game, you never shuffle your deck. When your draw pile runs out, you flip your discard pile over and it becomes your new draw pile. The order you discard in determines what you draw in future turns.

walkthrough.title: No Shuffling Ever
walkthrough.text: Let's say that again because it is the most important rule in the game. You never shuffle your personal deck. When your draw pile is empty, flip your entire discard pile face down and it becomes your new draw pile. That means if you discard a powerful spell last, it ends up on the bottom of your new draw pile, and you will draw it last. Discard strategically. Put the cards you want to see soon on top of your discard pile by discarding them last.

walkthrough.title: Nemesis Turn — Main Phase
walkthrough.text: When a nemesis turn order card is flipped, the nemesis acts. The nemesis turn has two phases: Main Phase and Strike Phase. During the Main Phase, draw the top card of the nemesis deck and resolve it. There are three types of nemesis cards: attacks, minions, and powers. Each type works differently and threatens us in a unique way.

walkthrough.title: Nemesis Cards — Attacks
walkthrough.text: Attack cards resolve immediately when drawn. They deal damage to mages, Gravehold, or both, and then go to the nemesis discard pile. Attacks are painful but predictable. You take the hit, you deal with it, and you move on. Some attacks target the player with the most life, the least life, or all players at once. Read the card carefully and resolve exactly what it says.

walkthrough.title: Nemesis Cards — Minions
walkthrough.text: Minions are persistent threats. When a minion card is drawn, place it face up in the nemesis play area. It stays there and activates every nemesis Strike Phase until we destroy it. Minions have their own hit points. We can target them with our spells instead of the nemesis. Ignoring a minion means it keeps dealing damage turn after turn. Prioritizing which minions to kill is a constant team decision.

walkthrough.title: Nemesis Cards — Powers
walkthrough.text: Powers are countdown threats. When a power card is drawn, place it in the nemesis play area with its power tokens. Each nemesis Strike Phase, remove one token. When the last token is removed, the power resolves with a devastating effect and is discarded. We can see what is coming and how many turns we have. Some powers can be discarded early by dealing enough damage to them. Powers force us to plan ahead or suffer the consequences.

walkthrough.title: Nemesis Turn — Strike Phase
walkthrough.text: After the nemesis Main Phase, the Strike Phase happens. All minions in play activate their effects, dealing damage or causing other harm. All powers in play lose one token, ticking closer to detonation. The nemesis itself may also have a persistent ability that triggers during Strike. The Strike Phase is where the pain piles up. Multiple minions and powers stacking together can create devastating turns.

walkthrough.title: Mage Exhaustion
walkthrough.text: Each mage has their own hit points. When a mage takes damage, reduce their life. If a mage reaches zero, they are exhausted. An exhausted mage is out of the game. They cannot take turns, prep spells, or use abilities. If all mages are exhausted, we lose immediately. Keeping each other alive is critical. Some relics and charge abilities can heal mages, so use them wisely.

walkthrough.title: Winning and Losing
walkthrough.text: We win by reducing the nemesis to zero hit points. We lose if Gravehold reaches zero hit points or if all mages are exhausted. It is a race. The nemesis is constantly wearing us down while we build up our decks to deal enough damage. Early turns are about survival and deck building. Late turns are about unleashing everything we have. The tension between building and fighting is what makes every game feel like a desperate last stand.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Gravehold
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Aeon's End. We are the breach mages, the last defenders of Gravehold, and a nemesis is marching toward our city. In this practice tutorial, we will walk through several turns with two mages so you can see how deck building, breach management, and nemesis threats all come together. Player one is playing Adelheim, a support mage. Player two is playing Brama, a damage-focused mage. Our nemesis is Rageborne. Let's save our city.

walkthrough.title: The Starting Position
walkthrough.text: Both mages have their starting decks. Adelheim has drawn five cards: three Crystals and two Sparks. Brama has drawn four Crystals and one Spark. Each Crystal generates one Aether when played, and each Spark is a basic spell that deals one damage. Breach one is open on both mats. Breaches two through four are closed. Rageborne sits across the table at seventy hit points. Gravehold is at thirty. Let's flip the first turn order card.

walkthrough.title: Adelheim's First Turn
walkthrough.text: The first turn order card is a player turn. Adelheim goes first. Casting Phase: no spells prepped yet, so we skip it. Main Phase: Adelheim plays all three Crystals for three Aether. Now let's prep that Spark. Place it on Breach one. It will fire next turn for one damage. With three Aether, Adelheim buys a Jade from the gem supply, costing two Aether, and banks the remaining one Aether. Wait, we cannot bank Aether between turns. Any unspent Aether is lost. Let's spend it wisely.

walkthrough.title: Adelheim's Discard Decision
walkthrough.text: Adelheim moves to the Draw Phase. One Spark was prepped on Breach one, so it stays there. The three played Crystals and the unplayed Spark need to be discarded. Here is where the no-shuffle rule kicks in. Adelheim wants to draw the new Jade soon. But the Jade went to the discard pile when purchased. Let's think about discard order. Adelheim discards the unplayed Spark first, then the three Crystals on top. Then draws five new cards.

walkthrough.title: Nemesis Strikes First Time
walkthrough.text: The next turn order card is a nemesis turn. During the nemesis Main Phase, we draw a nemesis card. It is an attack that deals two damage to Gravehold. Gravehold drops from thirty to twenty-eight. Then the Strike Phase: no minions or powers in play yet, so nothing else happens. That was manageable. But Rageborne is just warming up. The tier one cards are the gentlest. Tier three will be much worse.

walkthrough.title: Brama's First Turn
walkthrough.text: Another player turn is flipped. Brama takes over. Casting Phase: no prepped spells, skip it. Main Phase: Brama plays all four Crystals for four Aether. That is enough to buy an Ignite from the spell supply, costing four Aether. Ignite deals three damage when cast. Brama also preps the Spark on Breach one. Good. Next turn, that Spark fires for one damage. Draw Phase: Brama discards the four played Crystals and draws five.

walkthrough.title: Brama's Mistake 1 — Careless Discards
walkthrough.text: Let's pause and look at what Brama just did. Brama discarded those four Crystals in a random pile without thinking about the order. Remember, there is no shuffling. When Brama's draw pile runs out and the discard flips over, the order of those cards is locked in. Brama just put the newly purchased Ignite somewhere in the middle of a stack of Crystals with no thought about when it will show up.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Stack Your Discards with Purpose
walkthrough.text: The discard order is your most powerful tool. When you discard at the end of your turn, think about what you want to draw together on a future turn. Put cards you want to see soon on top of the discard pile by discarding them last. If Brama had discarded three Crystals first and then the Ignite last, the Ignite would be near the top of the next cycle. Pair your damage spells with enough gems to still buy cards. Every discard is a decision.

walkthrough.title: Round Two Begins
walkthrough.text: We reshuffle the turn order deck. Wait, the turn order deck does get shuffled each round, unlike our personal decks. Let's flip the first card. It is a nemesis turn. The nemesis draws a card from its deck, and this time it is a minion: a Rageborne Brute with five hit points. Place it in the nemesis play area. The Strike Phase activates, and the Brute immediately deals one damage to the mage with the lowest life. Adelheim takes one damage.

walkthrough.title: Adelheim's Second Turn
walkthrough.text: Next flip is a player turn. Adelheim's Spark is prepped on Breach one. Casting Phase: the Spark fires and deals one damage. But where do we aim it? Adelheim fires it at Rageborne, dealing one damage to the nemesis. Rageborne drops from seventy to sixty-nine. That Brute minion is still sitting there. It will deal damage again next Strike Phase. Adelheim plays gems, buys another card, preps a spell, and discards carefully this time.

walkthrough.title: Brama's Mistake 2 — Ignoring the Minion
walkthrough.text: Brama's turn comes up. Brama's Spark fires during the Casting Phase. Brama aims it at Rageborne again. One more damage to the boss. But the Brute is still alive with five hit points and it deals one damage to a mage every single Strike Phase. Over the next three nemesis turns, the Brute will deal three more damage to our mages. Brama's one damage to Rageborne barely scratched a seventy-hit-point boss, while the Brute keeps chewing through our health.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Kill Minions Before They Snowball
walkthrough.text: Minions deal recurring damage every nemesis Strike Phase. The longer a minion lives, the more total damage it deals. That Brute with five hit points will deal one damage per Strike Phase for as long as it survives. If it lives for five nemesis turns, that is five damage we could have prevented by killing it early. Always evaluate whether a minion's accumulated damage will exceed the benefit of hitting the nemesis directly. Most of the time, kill the minion first.

walkthrough.title: Adelheim Opens Breach Two
walkthrough.text: Adelheim has been saving up. Over two turns, Adelheim focused Breach two once and now pays the open cost to flip it face up permanently. Adelheim now has two open breaches. That means Adelheim can prep two spells at the same time, doubling the damage output each Casting Phase. This is a huge upgrade. Breaches are expensive to open, but the payoff lasts the entire game. Adelheim preps a spell on each open breach.

walkthrough.title: A Power Card Appears
walkthrough.text: The nemesis draws a power card: Cataclysmic Force. It enters play with three power tokens. Each Strike Phase, one token is removed. When the last token is removed, it deals seven damage to Gravehold. That would drop Gravehold from twenty-four to seventeen. We have three nemesis Strike Phases to either brace for the hit or find a way to discard the power early. The clock is ticking.

walkthrough.title: Brama's Mistake 3 — All Damage, No Economy
walkthrough.text: Brama finally draws that Ignite spell and preps it. But over the last few turns, Brama has been buying only spells. Brama's deck is full of Sparks and Ignites but still running on basic Crystals for Aether generation. Brama plays two Crystals for two Aether. That is only enough to focus a breach or buy the cheapest gem. Brama cannot afford any of the powerful four-cost or five-cost spells in the supply. Brama's damage is capped because the economy never grew.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Upgrade Your Economy Early
walkthrough.text: Gems are the engine of your deck. Basic Crystals generate one Aether each. Upgraded gems generate two or three. If you never buy better gems, you are stuck with one Aether per card for the entire game while spell costs keep climbing. Buy at least one or two upgraded gems in the first few rounds. A single three-Aether gem replaces three Crystals worth of buying power. Build your economy first, then invest in the big spells that will actually take down the nemesis.

walkthrough.title: The Team Adapts
walkthrough.text: Now our mages start playing smarter. Brama buys a Jade to boost economy. Adelheim focuses on support, using relics to heal Gravehold and charging up for a powerful ability. They coordinate: Adelheim will handle the Brute minion while Brama builds toward big damage against Rageborne. They start discarding with purpose, stacking gems and spells together so they draw powerful hands.

walkthrough.title: The Power Detonates
walkthrough.text: Cataclysmic Force loses its last token and detonates. Seven damage slams into Gravehold, dropping it to seventeen. It hurts, but we planned for it. Adelheim had already healed Gravehold with a relic last turn, giving us the buffer to survive. If we had not prepared, Gravehold would be dangerously low. Powers are telegraphed threats. Use the countdown turns to heal, buy defensive relics, or find ways to prevent the damage.

walkthrough.title: Brama Unleashes
walkthrough.text: With an upgraded economy and two breaches now open, Brama preps Ignite and a newly purchased four-damage spell across both breaches. Casting Phase: three damage plus four damage. Seven damage to Rageborne in a single turn. The nemesis drops into the forties. That is the power of a well-built deck. Adelheim's charge ability fires too, dealing bonus damage. Together they chip away at Rageborne's remaining health. The tide is turning.

walkthrough.title: What We Learned
walkthrough.text: Let's recap the three critical lessons from this practice game. First, always discard in a deliberate order because your discard pile becomes your next draw pile without shuffling. Second, kill minions early because their recurring damage adds up fast and outpaces chipping the boss. Third, invest in your economy by buying upgraded gems so you can afford the powerful spells and breach openings that win the game. These three principles will guide every decision you make.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations on completing your first practice game of Aeon's End. You have seen how the turn order deck creates tension, how breach management unlocks your damage potential, and how the no-shuffle discard mechanic rewards careful planning. You watched Brama learn from three mistakes and become a powerhouse. You saw Adelheim support the team with healing and breach investment. Most importantly, you experienced what makes this game special: working together under pressure to save Gravehold.

walkthrough.title: Before Your Next Battle
walkthrough.text:
Before you face the nemesis again, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for breach management priorities and economy pacing.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to card types, breach mechanics, and key terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q and A tab and ask any question during your game. Gravehold is counting on you, and you do not have to defend it alone.

summary.title: Welcome to Gravehold
bullets: Cooperative tutorial with 2 mages: Adelheim (support) and Brama (damage)
Nemesis: Rageborne (70 HP)
Covers deck building, breaches, nemesis threats, discard strategy

Tier 1 cards are mild — escalates later

bullets: 4 Crystals → 4 Aether → buy Ignite (3 dmg spell)
Prep Spark on Breach I
Ignite goes to discard pile (drawn later)

summary.title: Brama's Mistake 1 — Careless Discards
bullets: MISTAKE: Discarded cards in random order
Ignite buried randomly in deck cycle
No plan for when key cards will be drawn

bullets: Discard what you want soonest LAST (goes on top)
Pair spells with gems for strong future hands

Nemesis draws Brute minion (5 HP, 1 dmg/Strike Phase)

Adelheim buys cards, preps spells, discards carefully

summary.title: Brama's Mistake 2 — Ignoring the Minion
bullets: MISTAKE: Aimed Spark at Rageborne instead of Brute
1 dmg to a 70 HP boss is negligible
Brute deals 1 dmg per Strike Phase — accumulates fast

summary.title: Lesson: Kill Minions Before They Snowball
bullets: Minions deal recurring damage every Strike Phase
Brute alive 5 turns = 5 total damage (more than boss chip)
Kill minions first, then focus nemesis

summary.title: Adelheim Opens Breach Two
bullets: Focused Breach II → paid open cost → permanently open
2 open breaches = 2 spells prepped per turn
Expensive but lasts entire game

summary.title: A Power Card Appears
bullets: Cataclysmic Force: 3 tokens, deals 7 dmg to Gravehold when detonates
Loses 1 token per Strike Phase — 3-turn countdown
Must prepare: heal Gravehold, buy defensive relics, or discard power early

summary.title: Brama's Mistake 3 — All Damage, No Economy
bullets: MISTAKE: Bought only spells, never upgraded gems
Still running on 1-Aether Crystals
Cannot afford powerful spells or breach openings

summary.title: Lesson: Upgrade Your Economy Early
bullets: Buy 1-2 upgraded gems early (2-3 Aether each)
1 upgraded gem replaces multiple Crystals
Economy first → big spells later

summary.title: The Team Adapts
bullets: Brama buys Jade for economy, Adelheim handles support/healing
Coordinate roles: support mage + damage mage
Discard with purpose — stack gems + spells together

summary.title: The Power Detonates
bullets: Cataclysmic Force fires → 7 dmg to Gravehold (→ 17 HP)
Pre-healing absorbed some impact
Powers are telegraphed — use countdown turns to prepare

summary.title: Brama Unleashes
bullets: 2 breaches open → Ignite (3 dmg) + new spell (4 dmg) = 7 dmg/turn
Upgraded economy funds ongoing purchases
Adelheim charge ability adds bonus damage

summary.title: What We Learned
bullets: Lesson 1: Discard deliberately — order determines future draws
Lesson 2: Kill minions early — recurring damage snowballs
Lesson 3: Upgrade economy — basic gems can't fund late-game spells

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: Covered turn order tension, breach management, no-shuffle mechanic
Brama's 3 mistakes → 3 core lessons
Teamwork between support and damage mages

summary.title: Before Your Next Battle
bullets: Check General Tips for breach priorities + economy pacing
Use Appendix for card types, breach mechanics, key terms
Ask GM Guide anytime via Q&A tab

walkthrough.title: Master the Discard Order
walkthrough.text: This is the single most important skill in the game. Every time you discard, think about what you want your next hand to look like. If you know your draw pile has three cards left, the two cards you discard first will be the last two you draw in your next cycle. Pair gems with spells so you draw hands that can both generate Aether and prep powerful attacks. Never discard mindlessly.

walkthrough.title: Buy Economy Before Firepower
walkthrough.text: In the first two rounds, prioritize buying at least one upgraded gem. A single gem that generates two or three Aether dramatically accelerates your deck. If you rush spells without upgrading gems, you will draw hands full of damage that you cannot supplement with purchases or breach openings. Think of gems as the foundation and spells as the building on top. Without a strong foundation, your damage output will plateau.

walkthrough.title: Open Breaches Aggressively
walkthrough.text: An open breach is the gift that keeps giving for the entire game. Two open breaches means two spells firing every Casting Phase. Three open breaches is devastating. Focus and open your second breach as early as you can afford it. Yes, it costs Aether that could buy cards, but every turn after you open it, you prep an extra spell. The math works overwhelmingly in your favor over the course of a full game.

walkthrough.title: Communicate and Coordinate
walkthrough.text: Let's remember we are all on the same team. Talk about what you are planning. Share what is in your hand. Discuss who should target the minion and who should hit the boss. If a power card is counting down, decide together who is best positioned to deal with it. Coordinate your charge abilities so they fire when they matter most. Two mages working in sync are far more effective than two mages playing their own game.

walkthrough.title: Respect the Turn Order Deck
walkthrough.text: The nemesis can go twice before you act. Plan for the worst case. If Gravehold is low, save a healing relic in your hand in case the nemesis gets back-to-back turns. If a minion is about to attack, make sure you have already prepped a spell to kill it before another Strike Phase. The uncertainty of turn order means you should always leave yourself a safety margin rather than going all-in on offense.

walkthrough.title: Kill Minions and Watch Power Timers
walkthrough.text: Minions and powers are the nemesis cards that beat teams. An unchecked minion deals damage every single Strike Phase. A power card counts down to a devastating blast. Always do the math. If a minion deals two damage per turn and has six hit points, it will deal eight or more damage before you can kill it if you wait. Kill it now. For powers, count the remaining tokens and plan your response during those countdown turns.

walkthrough.title: Keep Your Deck Lean
walkthrough.text: In a deck builder with no shuffling, every card you add dilutes your cycle timing. Do not buy cards just because you can. A focused eight-card deck that cycles quickly draws your best cards every other turn. A bloated fifteen-card deck means your Ignite might not show up for three full cycles. Buy only what genuinely improves your deck. Quality over quantity. If a card will not meaningfully help, save that Aether for a breach or a charge.

walkthrough.title: Protect Gravehold as a Team
walkthrough.text: Gravehold has thirty hit points and no way to dodge incoming damage. The nemesis will chip away at it throughout the game. Some players focus entirely on nemesis damage and forget about city defense until it is too late. Healing Gravehold is a shared responsibility. If a relic or charge ability can restore city health, use it before things get critical. Losing because Gravehold hit zero while the nemesis had three health left is the most painful way to lose.

bullets: Discard deliberately — order = future draw order
Pair gems + spells for balanced future hands
Never discard mindlessly

Focus + open Breach II ASAP
Long-term payoff far exceeds short-term Aether cost

Decide together: who handles minion vs. boss
Sync charge abilities for maximum impact

Count power tokens — plan response during countdown

summary.title: Keep Your Deck Lean
bullets: No shuffling = cycle timing matters
Focused 8-card deck cycles fast, bloated 15-card deck is slow
Buy only meaningful upgrades — quality over quantity

summary.title: Protect Gravehold as a Team
bullets: Gravehold = 30 HP, no dodging — chip damage adds up
Healing Gravehold is shared responsibility
Losing at Gravehold 0 with nemesis at 3 HP = worst loss

walkthrough.title: Cycle Mapping
walkthrough.text: Advanced players track exactly where every card is in their deck cycle. Count your draw pile, count your discard pile, and know when your best card is coming. If your Ignite is three cards deep in the draw pile, you know it arrives in your next hand. This lets you plan two turns ahead. When you combine cycle mapping with deliberate discarding, you can engineer exactly the hand you need for a critical turn.

walkthrough.title: Breach Sequencing
walkthrough.text: The order you open breaches matters more than you think. Breach three and four have higher focus and open costs than Breach two. But some mages have unique breach configurations where Breach three is cheaper or starts partially focused. Always check the costs on each closed breach before deciding which to open next. Sometimes skipping Breach two and going straight to three saves Aether if three has a lower total cost for your specific mage.

walkthrough.title: Strategic Nemesis Targeting
walkthrough.text: Experienced teams split their focus deliberately. One mage becomes the minion killer, focusing on smaller spells that can surgically remove threats each turn. The other mage becomes the boss burner, building toward maximum damage output against the nemesis. This division of labor prevents wasted overkill on small minions and ensures consistent pressure on the boss. Assign roles based on mage abilities, starting breaches, and charge powers.

walkthrough.title: Charge Ability Timing
walkthrough.text: Do not just fire your charge ability the moment it is full. Some charge abilities are more valuable at specific moments. A healing charge ability is best saved for when Gravehold is under twenty. A damage charge ability is best held for a turn when you can combine it with multiple prepped spells for a devastating burst. Charging costs two Aether per charge point, so invest only when you have a clear plan for when and how you will use the ability.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Nemesis Deck
walkthrough.text: As the nemesis deck progresses from tier one to tier three, the threats escalate dramatically. Pay attention to how many cards have been drawn from each tier. When you know tier two is about to start, tighten your defenses. When tier three cards start appearing, you need to be in a position to close out the game quickly. Track the ratio of attacks, minions, and powers you have seen. If three attacks have appeared but no powers yet, brace for a power card soon.

walkthrough.title: Supply Selection for Experienced Players
walkthrough.text: The nine supply piles you choose before the game dramatically shape the entire experience. Advanced players select supply cards that synergize with their chosen mages. If your mage has a damage charge ability, pick spells that deal moderate damage to complement it. If one mage focuses on support, include relics that heal or draw cards. Avoid picking three expensive spells with no affordable gems to fund them. The supply is a puzzle you solve before the game even begins.

walkthrough.title: Aether Efficiency and Waste Reduction
walkthrough.text: Unspent Aether vanishes at the end of your turn. Advanced players minimize waste by planning purchases before playing gems. Count the Aether in your hand first, check the supply costs, and decide what to buy before you start playing cards. If you have five Aether and the best purchase costs four, you have one Aether to spend elsewhere: a focus step on a breach or a charge point. Letting even one Aether go unspent each turn adds up to significant lost value over a full game.

walkthrough.title: The Endgame Sprint
walkthrough.text: When the nemesis is below twenty hit points, shift your entire strategy to maximum damage. Stop buying cards. Stop focusing breaches. Stop charging unless your charge ability deals damage. Every point of Aether should go toward actions that kill the nemesis this turn or next turn. The endgame is a race because tier three nemesis cards can end the game fast. Coordinate your final burst: both mages should have their strongest spells prepped and ready to fire on back-to-back turns.

entries.definition: Basic starting gem card. Generates 1 Aether when played during the Main Phase.
Basic starting spell card. Deals 1 damage when cast during the Casting Phase.
Card type that generates Aether when played. Played from hand during Main Phase. Core economy cards.
Card type that deals damage or triggers effects. Must be prepped on an open breach, then casts automatically next Casting Phase.
Card type with one-time effects. Played from hand during Main Phase. Effects include healing, card draw, bonus damage, etc.
Nemesis card that resolves immediately when drawn. Deals damage to mages, Gravehold, or both, then is discarded.
Nemesis card that persists in play until destroyed. Has HP and activates each Strike Phase, dealing recurring damage.
Nemesis card with a countdown. Enters play with power tokens; loses one per Strike Phase. Devastating effect when last token removed.
Slot on a mage mat where spells are prepped. Each mage has 4 breaches. Only open breaches can hold spells.
A breach that is face down and cannot hold spells. Must be focused and then opened to become usable.
A closed breach that has been rotated one or more steps toward open. Pay the focus cost during Main Phase to rotate it.
A breach that is face up and permanently available. Can hold one prepped spell at a time. Breach I starts open for most mages.
The Aether cost to rotate a closed breach one step closer to open. Each breach shows its focus cost on the closed side.
The Aether cost to flip a fully focused breach to the open (face-up) position permanently.
Placing a spell card from your hand onto an open breach during your Main Phase. The spell fires next Casting Phase.
The boss enemy all players fight cooperatively. Has its own HP dial, deck of cards, and sometimes unique rules. Defeat it to win.
Three-tiered deck of nemesis cards (Tier 1 on top, Tier 3 on bottom). One card drawn per nemesis Main Phase. Escalates in difficulty.
The nemesis's life total, tracked on a dial. Reducing it to zero wins the game. Starting HP varies by nemesis.
Tokens placed on nemesis power cards. One removed per Strike Phase. When the last is removed, the power's effect resolves.
First phase of a player turn. All prepped spells fire automatically, dealing damage. Spells then go to the discard pile.
Second phase of a player turn. Play gems/relics, prep spells, focus/open breaches, buy cards from supply, gain charges.
Third phase of a player turn. Discard remaining hand in chosen order, then draw 5 cards. Discard order determines future draws.
First phase of a nemesis turn. Draw and resolve the top card of the nemesis deck (attack, minion, or power).
Second phase of a nemesis turn. All minions activate, all powers lose one token, and the nemesis persistent ability may trigger.
Shuffled deck of player and nemesis turn cards. Flipped one at a time to determine who acts next. Reshuffled each round.
The game's currency, generated by playing gem cards. Spent to buy supply cards, focus/open breaches, and gain charges. Unspent Aether is lost at end of turn.
The last city of humanity. Starts at 30 HP. If reduced to 0, all players lose. Some effects heal Gravehold.
The 9 card piles available for purchase: 3 gems, 3 relics, 3 spells. Chosen during setup. Defines available strategy options.
A mage reduced to 0 HP is exhausted and out of the game. If all mages are exhausted, the team loses immediately.
Face-up pile of played/discarded cards. When draw pile is empty, flip the entire discard pile face down to form a new draw pile. Never shuffled.
One complete pass through your personal deck. From full draw pile to empty, then discard flips over. No shuffling means the cycle order is controllable.
Unique power on each mage mat. Spend 2 Aether to gain 1 charge. When the charge track is full, activate for a powerful one-time effect.
Track on each mage mat showing charge progress. Number of charges needed varies by mage (typically 4-5). Resets after ability is used.
Player board showing the mage's 4 breaches, charge track, charge ability, life total, and special rules.
The preset personal deck each mage begins with. Typically basic Crystals and Sparks, but varies by mage. Listed on the mage card.
A passive or triggered power unique to each mage, printed on their mage card. Active throughout the game without spending charges.
